misc.title: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 This song may actually be hinting at Laugh Tale. “The edge of the map begins to shine.” “This place isn’t something you can just take so easily.” “In
misc.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 This song may actually be hinting at Laugh Tale. “The edge of the map begins to shine.” “This place isn’t something you can just take so easily.” “In the sea of carnage, even the authorities are too scared to come near. There are stories about people like that.” The entire song Brook sang this time is written in Kansai dialect, a regional dialect used in areas including Osaka and Kyoto. In One Piece, it was explained as the dialect of a country called カミガタ Kamigata. “カミガタ Kamigata” was originally a term used during Japan’s Edo period, when the country was closed off from the outside world. It referred to the Kinki region centered around Kyoto and Osaka. The name comes from the idea of “the direction of the upper place,” meaning the capital where the Emperor lived. Originally, Japan’s Emperor was in Kamigata, but now he is in Tokyo. In the same way, this may be hinting that in the world of One Piece, there was once a true “kami,” or god, in Kamigata, but now a false god is sitting in Mariejois.

items.title: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 This song may actually be hinting at Laugh Tale. “The edge of the map begins to shine.” “This place isn’t something you can just take so easily.” “In
items.text: 【Lost in Translation by a Native Japanese Speaker】 This song may actually be hinting at Laugh Tale. “The edge of the map begins to shine.” “This place isn’t something you can just take so easily.” “In the sea of carnage, even the authorities are too scared to come near. There are stories about people like that.” The entire song Brook sang this time is written in Kansai dialect, a regional dialect used in areas including Osaka and Kyoto. In One Piece, it was explained as the dialect of a country called カミガタ Kamigata. “カミガタ Kamigata” was originally a term used during Japan’s Edo period, when the country was closed off from the outside world. It referred to the Kinki region centered around Kyoto and Osaka. The name comes from the idea of “the direction of the upper place,” meaning the capital where the Emperor lived. Originally, Japan’s Emperor was in Kamigata, but now he is in Tokyo. In the same way, this may be hinting that in the world of One Piece, there was once a true “kami,” or god, in Kamigata, but now a false god is sitting in Mariejois.
